content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 20,
1994
Sabina D. Gilbert
City Attorney
City of Rocklin
3970 Rocklin Road
P.O.
Box 1138
Rocklin,
CA
95677
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-319
Dear Ms. Gilbert:
This is in response to your letter requesting follow—up
advice on behalf of Councilmember Ken Yorde, regarding his
responsibilities as a Rocklin City Councilmember under the
conflict-of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”) .
This letter is intended to supplement the advice
provided in January 1993,
in the Yorde Advice Letter,
No.
A-93—
486.
QUESTIONS
If Councilmember Yorde abstains from participating in
decisions concerning the specific aspects of the proposed North
Rocklin Circulation Element,
may the councilmember participate in
the final adoption of the element?
CONCLUSIONS
To the extent that the coüncilmember has a conflict of
interest with respect to any component of the North Rocklin
Circulation Element, the councilinember is disqualified from
participating in any decision that can result in a reopening or in
any way affect the decisions from which the councilmember is
disqualified.
This includes adoption of the North Rocklin
Circulation Element.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—94—319
Page
2
FACTS
You stated that the facts were the same as provided in
connection with our initial advice letter to Councilmember Yorde.
The councilmember and his spouse own a home in the jurisdiction,
located at 4335 Midas Avenue, Rocklin.
They also own a ten—acre
parcel of undeveloped land located in the unincorporated territory
of Placer County, but within the City of Rocklin’s sphere of
influence.
The property is part of a 137 lot subdivision known as
Sunset Ranchos Estates Unit 1 and is located in the northern most
portion of the subdivision,
immediately adjacent to the outer
boundary of Rocklin’s sphere of influence and approximately 4,000
feet from the city’s border.
The present value of the lot is over $100,000.
The City of
Rocklin is currently considering the North Rocklin Circulation
Element.
The project consists of the revisions of the circulation
element of the Rocklin General Plan as it relates to the North
Rocklin area of the city which includes over 85 percent of the
total land area of the city.
The project also covers portions of
the unincorporated territory within the city’s sphere of
influence, including the area where the lot is located.
A chief
objective of the project is to design a circulation system which
will accommodate development within the city and its sphere of
influence to the year 2020.
The project has two components.
The first is the Base
Circulation Plan which would accommodate the city’s projected
growth to the year 2020.
The improvements are recommended to
resolve anticipated level of service problems due to the projected
growth; to reduce traffic on certain key collector roadways with
residential frontage;
and to improve circulation in some high
growth areas of the city.
None of the proposed improvements are
located in the vicinity of the property.
However, approval of the
Base Circulation Plan, which would improve the overall circulation
system in the city,
would enhance the development potential of the
property.
The second component of the project is the Long Range
Planning Option.
This component was included in case the city
allowed higher densities in areas within its “planning reserve”
(sphere of influence),
such as the Sunset Ranchos.
It is not
anticipated that these improvements would be needed before the
year 2020.
These proposed improvements are located much closer to
the property (probably within 2,500 feet).
While the current
zoning in the Sunset Ranchos area allows for a development density
of one unit per ten acres, this project assumes a higher
development density of one unit per acre, anticipating that an
increase in density is likely before development occurs.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her


Our File No. A94-319
Page
3
official position to influence a governmental decision which will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any
economic interest.
As we discussed in our prior letter, real property owned by a
public official is a potentially disqualifying economic interest
under the Act if the official’s direct or indirect interest in the
property is $1,000 or more.
(Section 87103 (b).)
Thus, the
councilmember has an economic interest in both his undeveloped lot
and personal residence.
He is prohibited from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his official position
to influence a governmental decision which will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally,
on these two properties.
In our prior letter,
we discussed the standards for
foreseeability and materiality.
You stated that, based on the
standards set out in Regulation 18702.3,
Councilmember Yorde will
abstain from decisions on long range planning around his
undeveloped property.
You now ask whether he is permitted to vote on the final
adoption of North Rocklin Circulation Element as a whole.
Because
he has a financial conflict of interest in the decision, the
councilmember may participate in this decision only if one of the
following exceptions apply:
o
“Public Generally” Exception:
As stated in the prior
letter, Regulation 18703 provides an exception to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act if the effect on the official’s
interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.
Regulation 18703 provides that the “public generally”
exception applies where both subdivisions
(1)
and
(2)
apply.
(1)
significant Segment:
The governmental
decision will affect a “significant segment” of the
public generally as set forth below:
(A)
The decision will affect:
(i)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents, or
(ii)
Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners, or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents, or


Our File No.
A-94—319
Page 4
(iii)
Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents,
so long
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade, or
profession; or,
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,
(2)
Substantially the Same Manner:
The
governmental decision will affect the official’s
economic interest in substantially the same manner
as it will affect the economic interests of the
segment identified in subdivision (a) (1) of this
regulation.
Thus, to the extent this exception applies with respect to
adoption of the circulation element, the councilmember may
participate in the decision.
o
Presumptions:
Under some circumstances, decisions affect
such a vast portion of the population that we have presumed that
the official will not be affected significantly differently.
For
example,
in the McAndrews Advice Letter,
No. I-94-215
we advised
that a public official may vote on the adoption of the final
budget, despite the fact that the final budget includes items from
which the public official was disqualified.
This was the case so
long as the final budget decision could not result in a reopening
or in any way change the decisions from which the public official
was previously disqualified.
This was because the adoption of an
agency’s budget was a type of decision that had such a widespread
and diffused effect.
In addition,
we have advised that once all the specific
decisions related to a general plan have been finalized, the final
vote to adopt or reject the plan will not require disqualification
so long as the plan is not modified at that time.
(Marino Advice
Letter,
No. 1-89-291.)
This is the case because the general plan,
as implemented by the various elements included within, will
affect the public official involved in a manner which is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
However,
we have never advised that a single element of the
general plan falls within such a presumption.
“Generally, the
Commission will refrain from providing per se exceptions to the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.
While under some
factual scenarios the effect on a public official from a specific
element of the general plan will be the same as the effect on the
public generally, it does not appear that this will always be the
case.”
(Krauel Advice Letter,
No.
1—92—283.)


Our File No. A-94-319
Page 5
o
Seqmentation of Decisions:
As we advised previously,
decisions may be segmented so that the officials may participate
in some aspects of the plan that will not affect their economic
interests.
Where decisions can be segmented, the following
procedure may be used to permit the officials to participate in
other decisions:
1.
The decisions for which the officials have a
disqualifying financial interest must be segregated from the other
decisions;
2.
The decisions for which the officials are disqualified
must be considered first,
and a final decision reached by their
agencies without their participation;
3.
Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
North Rocklin Circulation Element for which the officials have a
disqualifying interest, the officials may participate in the
subsequent deliberations regarding other portions of the North
Rocklin Circulation Element,
so long as:
(1) those deliberations
do not result in a reopening or in any way affect the decision
from which the officials were disqualified, and
(2) those
decisions will not have a material financial effect on the
officials’ economic interest.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No.
A-86—
343.)
However, under some circumstances, a series of decisions may
be too interrelated to be considered separately.
(Miller Advice
Letter,
No. A-82-119.)
Under your facts, the underlying decisions.
within the traffic circulation element cannot be segregated from
the adoption of the element.
A vote against the element will
effectively nullify the prior decision from which the
councilmember was disqualified.
A vote for the element will
ratify the decisions.
It does not appear from your facts that the
adoption of the element is separable from the decision for which
the councilmember has a conf1ict of interest.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
Counsel
By: \John W. Wallace
Counsel, Lea1 Division

decisions concerning the specific aspects of the proposed North
Rocklin Circulation Element,
may the councilmember participate in
the final adoption of the element?

CONCLUSION: To the extent that the coüncilmember has a conflict of
interest with respect to any component of the North Rocklin
Circulation Element, the councilinember is disqualified from
participating in any decision that can result in a reopening or in
any way affect the decisions from which the councilmember is
disqualified.
This includes adoption of the North Rocklin
Circulation Element.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
